findings.finding: The plan asserts _SCAN_MAX_FILES is 'defined + documented but never enforced in search_files', treating it as a dead constant safe to remove. In fact, pai_tools.search_files imports it from fs_tools and enforces it at lines 126-130 (the scan-limit truncation path). Removing it from fs_tools.py without updating pai_tools.py and those tests would break the scan-limit feature and the identity-contract tests.

test_fs_tools_extraction.py (two assertions) and test_llm_framework.py (import sanity) fail immediately.

Or, keep it in fs_tools.py (where it currently serves as the single source of truth for pai_tools) and remove the finding from the plan entirely.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the canonical `run()` signature (required or optional `agent_runner`) and note which class is the source-of-truth, so 'aligned' has an unambiguous observable meaning.
evidence: 'base requires agent_runner, override makes it optional' — describes current mismatch but not which side changes
AC only says 'signatures aligned', not which signature is the canonical one
The two possible resolutions (require in both vs. optional in both) expose callers to different type errors
findings.finding: The runners.py AC ('align the base/override run() signature') does not specify the direction of alignment — whether the base relaxes agent_runner to optional (matching override) or the override tightens it to required (matching base) — leaving the implementer free to build either contract, which are non-equivalent and affect callers differently.
findings.location: Scope › Tier 2 › runners.py (#450); AC line 'runners.py signatures aligned'
scenarios: Implementer makes agent_runner optional in the base — existing callers that rely on agent_runner always being set may get runtime None; mypy may not catch it depending on downstream use.
Implementer makes agent_runner required in the override — any site calling the override without agent_runner breaks at runtime.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the AC: 'The base class signature is canonical; the override is updated to also require agent_runner (i.e., both require it), and any call site currently relying on the override's default is updated to pass the argument explicitly.' Or the reverse, but make the direction explicit.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify the runners.py alignment direction: specify whether the base `BatchRunner.run()` signature should be widened to `agent_runner: AgentStepRunner | None = None` (to match the existing overrides) or whether the overrides should be tightened — and state the positive rationale for the chosen direction.
evidence: src/rebar/llm/workflow/runners.py:94: def run(self, req: BatchRunRequest, agent_runner: AgentStepRunner) -> BatchRunResult:  # base — required
src/rebar/llm/plan_review/production_batch_runner.py:69: self, req: BatchRunRequest, agent_runner: AgentStepRunner | None = None  # override — optional
src/rebar/llm/code_review/batch_runner.py:39: def run(self, req: BatchRunRequest, agent_runner: Any) -> BatchRunResult:  # second override — Any

ABSENCE: plan does not state whether alignment means 'make base optional' (correct LSP: widen to match overrides) or 'make overrides required' (breaks ProductionBatchRunner which explicitly documents ignoring agent_runner)
findings.finding: The runners.py signature-alignment direction is ambiguous: the base `BatchRunner.run()` requires `agent_runner` while `ProductionBatchRunner.run()` already makes it optional — the plan says 'base requires, override makes it optional' but does not state which direction the alignment goes, risking an LSP-violating fix (making base optional while override stays required) or the wrong direction entirely.
findings.location: Scope / Tier 2 — runners.py (#450): 'align the base/override run() signature (base requires agent_runner, override makes it optional)'
scenarios: Implementer makes base optional (AgentStepRunner | None = None) — correct LSP fix, but must also update CodeReviewBatchRunner's Any annotation
Implementer makes ProductionBatchRunner required — breaks its documented design (it ignores agent_runner and uses its own Runner) and the del agent_runner guard
findings.suggested_fix: The LSP-correct direction is to widen the base: `def run(self, req: BatchRunRequest, agent_runner: AgentStepRunner | None = None) -> BatchRunResult`. This matches all existing overrides (ProductionBatchRunner already uses this; CodeReviewBatchRunner uses Any, which should be tightened to AgentStepRunner | None). State this explicitly in the plan.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add to the runners.py scope bullet (or AC) the explicit alignment direction: either (a) relax the base `BatchRunner.run()` to `agent_runner: AgentStepRunner | None = None` matching the override, or (b) document that `ProductionBatchRunner` is the exception and only the mypy/type-check error is suppressed.
evidence: runners.py line 94: `def run(self, req: BatchRunRequest, agent_runner: AgentStepRunner) -> BatchRunResult`  (base, required)
production_batch_runner.py line 69: `def run(self, req: BatchRunRequest, agent_runner: AgentStepRunner | None = None) -> BatchRunResult`  (override, optional)
gate_dispatch.py lines 173, 601, 670: all callers pass agent_runner as a positional/keyword non-None argument
ABSENCE: no plan statement specifying whether the base relaxes to Optional or the override tightens to required
findings.finding: The `runners.py` AC ('align the base/override run() signature') does not specify the direction of alignment: the base `BatchRunner.run()` requires `agent_runner: AgentStepRunner` (non-optional); the override `ProductionBatchRunner.run()` declares `agent_runner: AgentStepRunner | None = None` (optional). The plan names no decision rule for which end conforms to the other, leaving the executor without a protocol for resolving the seam — a critical decision for callers in `gate_dispatch.py` that currently always supply a non-None runner.
findings.location: Tier 1 scope bullet: 'runners.py (#450): align the base/override run() signature (base requires agent_runner, override makes it optional)'; AC: 'runners.py signatures aligned.'
scenarios: Executor makes base optional — all callers are technically fine but the base no longer expresses that a real agent runner is required for real work, weakening the contract.

Executor makes override required — callers that construct ProductionBatchRunner without an agent_runner (it intentionally ignores the arg) would need changes, but the code at line 73 (`del agent_runner`) shows it is deliberately optional there.

evidence: vague/subjective terms present: clean, improve
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Decide in the plan which branch applies for _HAVE_ASTGREP — either name the specific test(s) that need the guard, or state 'no ast-grep-dependent tests exist; remove the flag' — so the executor has a decided action, not a conditional to investigate.
evidence: 'wire the guard where an ast-grep-dependent test needs it, or remove the dead flag if none does'
ABSENCE: no enumeration of which tests depend on ast-grep, so the executor cannot pick a branch without investigation
findings.finding: The _HAVE_ASTGREP scope bullet defers a real design decision to the executor: it says 'wire the guard where an ast-grep-dependent test needs it, OR remove the dead flag if none does' without identifying which tests are ast-grep-dependent or which branch applies. The executor must investigate the test suite before acting, making this a placeholder conditional rather than a decided plan step.
scenarios: Executor spends time scanning for ast-grep-dependent tests and must make the design choice (keep vs delete) themselves, risking the wrong call with no plan guidance.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a parenthetical after the bullet naming the outcome of the investigation, e.g.: '…remove the dead flag (confirmed: no test in tests/unit/grounding currently requires ast-grep at runtime)' or '…wire the guard in test_foo.py::test_bar which calls ast-grep directly.'

scenarios: After the fix, a caller with ambient REBAR_SYNC_PULL=off invokes the reconciler via the run_differs path (e.g. _read_local_ticket_full); the live sync pull is still silently suppressed through the unpatched code path

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a plan step (or AC note) requiring the new regression test to be run against the pre-fix code to confirm it fails RED, then run again post-fix to confirm GREEN — e.g. 'stash the fix, run pytest tests/.../test_reconcile_helpers.py -k sync_pull_regression, confirm FAIL; unstash, rerun, confirm PASS'.
evidence: 'a regression test that fails before / passes after and asserts the live path is unaffected by an ambient REBAR_SYNC_PULL=off'
ABSENCE: no plan step instructs running the new test against the pre-fix code to produce the failing evidence
findings.finding: The AI-1 acceptance criterion asserts a 'fails before / passes after' regression test but no plan step describes how to demonstrate the RED (before) state. The AC is written as a correctness claim but the plan only says 'Add a regression test' in scope; it never instructs the executor to run the test against the unfixed code to confirm the red state. Without that step, the 'fails before' half of the AC is unverifiable from the plan.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria › AI-1 bullet; Scope › 'reconcile_helpers.py (AI-1)' bullet
scenarios: An executor writes a test that passes even on the unfixed code (e.g. because the test setup inadvertently masks the bug), and the 'fails before' criterion is never checked — the bug fix is unverified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify 'touched test modules pass' to mean the full repo test suite (or name the specific modules per Tier-2 deletion), and add at least one example grep pattern (e.g. `grep -rn '_RELATION_TO_JIRA_LINK' src/ tests/`) so the unreferenced-verification step is concrete and repeatable.
evidence: 'Each Tier-2 symbol verified unreferenced (grep) before deletion; behavior-preserving'
'make lint + mypy clean; touched test modules pass' — catch-all AC does not enumerate which modules are touched
ABSENCE: no grep command/pattern specified for the unreferenced check
findings.finding: The Tier-2 acceptance criterion 'Each Tier-2 symbol verified unreferenced (grep) before deletion; behavior-preserving' does not specify which test modules are expected to be touched by the deletions. Combined with no grep pattern guidance, 'behavior-preserving' is unanchored: the executor cannot know whether the catch-all 'touched test modules pass' covers all affected modules or only a subset.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria › Tier-2 bullet; Scope › Tier 2 list
scenarios: A Tier-2 deletion silently breaks a test in an untouched module (e.g. via import side-effects or a re-export) that the executor never reruns because it wasn't in the 'touched' set.
findings.suggested_fix: Change the AC to: 'Each Tier-2 symbol confirmed unreferenced via `grep -rn <symbol> src/ tests/` (zero hits outside the deletion site) before removal; full repo test suite passes after removal.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit rollback/back-out note for the runners.py signature change: confirm no external callers rely on the current required-positional form (repo-wide grep), and state the revert path (e.g., a separate CL or flag-gated shim) if post-merge breakage is found.
evidence: 'align the base/override run() signature (base requires agent_runner, override makes it optional)'
No rollback, back-out, or expand-contract language appears anywhere in the plan for this change.
AC only says 'runners.py signatures aligned' — no verification that existing callers are unaffected or that a revert path exists.
findings.finding: The runners.py signature alignment (making agent_runner optional in override vs. required in base) is an interface change on a base class method, but the plan neither acknowledges potential downstream breakage nor provides an expand-contract sequence or explicit rollback path.
scenarios: If any caller invokes the override path relying on the current (required) positional signature, aligning to optional breaks it silently at runtime. Without an explicit rollback plan, reverting requires a separate Gerrit CL under time pressure.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Scope entry: 'Verify no external callers pass agent_runner positionally to the override; if callers exist, use an expand-contract sequence (add default first, then remove the requirement in a follow-up). Revert path: rebase-and-abandon the single Gerrit CL.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Consider (advisory) whether AI-1 (live bug with regression test) should land ahead of the Tier-2 cleanup batch, to avoid a reliability fix being held up by unrelated dead-code deletions.
evidence: AI-1 (bug fix + regression test) has a distinct 'reason to change' from Tier-2 dead code removal
runners.py signature alignment is an API contract change, independently releasable from all other items
'single change' rationale in Context is a batching preference, not a coupling argument — no piece is order-dependent on another
findings.finding: The plan bundles four heterogeneous change kinds in one ticket — a behavior bug-fix with regression test (AI-1), test-coverage additions (AI-3b, _HAVE_ASTGREP), dead-symbol cleanup across multiple unrelated modules (Tier-2), and an API signature alignment (runners.py) — each of which is independently valuable, testable, and releasable.
findings.location: Scope › Tier 1 and Tier 2; entire plan
scenarios: A regression in the Tier-2 dead-symbol deletion blocks an urgent AI-1 bug-fix from landing.
A reviewer blocking runners.py alignment holds up the dead code cleanup.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
